text: I saw the dent in my car and the note on the windshield was blank. Just a blank piece of paper tucked under the wiper. Someone had smashed my taillight, crumpled the bumper, and left a blank note like a joke. My jaw clenched so tight my teeth ached. I crumpled the paper in my fist and stood there staring at the damage, heat rising up the back of my neck.

My coworker took credit for my project in the meeting. He presented my slides, used my data, even repeated the phrasing I had drafted. The boss congratulated him. I sat in my chair gripping the pen so hard it cracked. My face was burning. I wanted to stand up and say something but my throat was a fist. I stared at the back of his head and felt my pulse in my temples.

I opened the bank statement and saw three charges I never made. Someone had my card number. They spent four hundred dollars at a store I had never heard of. I called the bank and was put on hold for forty-five minutes. The hold music was cheerful. My leg was bouncing under the desk and I kept balling my free hand into a fist.

The landlord raised the rent by thirty percent and gave me two weeks to decide. Two weeks. I have lived here for six years. I fixed the plumbing myself. I painted every wall. I stood in my kitchen reading the letter and my hands were shaking, not from weakness. I wanted to tear the paper in half. I wanted to call him and say every word I had been holding back.

I watched the video of the officer pushing the old man to the ground. The man was eighty years old and he was bleeding from his head. The officers walked past him. I played it again and my chest was tight, my fists clenched on the table. I closed the laptop because if I watched it one more time I was going to throw something.

She lied to my face. I showed her the messages and she still denied it. I could feel something boiling behind my sternum. My voice dropped low and flat, the way it does when I am past yelling. I set the phone on the table between us and waited. She picked it up, saw the screenshots, and started crying. I did not feel sorry. I felt like a furnace with the door kicked open.

The insurance company denied the claim again. Third time. My mother needs the surgery and they sent another form letter about pre-existing conditions. I read the letter out loud to no one. My voice was tight and my hands were squeezing the paper into a tube. I called the number and got a recording and slammed the phone down so hard the screen cracked.

I spent three hours cooking a special dinner. I set the table with candles and real napkins. He came home two hours late, already full from takeout he grabbed with coworkers. He shrugged and said he forgot. I stood over the cold food with a spatula in my hand, my vision narrowing to a point. I scraped every plate into the trash without a word.

The referee made the worst call I have ever seen. The ball was clearly in. The whole stadium saw it. He just shook his head and reversed the point. I was on my feet screaming before I knew it. My voice cracked and my face was hot. The veins in my neck stood out. My friend put a hand on my arm and I shrugged it off.

I found out they gave the promotion to someone who had been there half as long as me. Someone I trained. My manager explained it with buzzwords about team dynamics and fresh perspectives. I sat across from him, perfectly still, my blood roaring in my ears. I thanked him, stood up, walked to the bathroom, and punched the paper towel dispenser.

Someone keyed my car. A long scratch running from the headlight to the rear quarter panel, deep enough to hit metal. I ran my finger along it and felt the raw groove. I looked around the parking lot like I might catch them. My breathing was fast and sharp. I stood there clenching and unclenching my fists, wanting someone to blame, needing a face to attach to this.

My neighbor played music until three in the morning for the fourth time this month. The bass thumped through my walls and rattled the picture frames. I lay in bed staring at the ceiling, grinding my teeth. I put in earplugs. I could still feel the vibration in my pillow. I got up and paced the apartment, heat spreading from my chest to my fingertips.

The contractor disappeared with our deposit. Twenty thousand dollars and three months of planning, gone. I called his number and it was disconnected. I drove to his office and it was empty. I stood on the sidewalk outside the locked door and the heat in my chest was so physical it felt like a second heartbeat. I kicked the door once. Then again. Then I sat on the curb and called a lawyer.

My brother borrowed my car and brought it back with an empty tank, fast food wrappers on the floor, and a cigarette burn on the seat. He did not mention any of it. Just tossed me the keys and said thanks. I looked at the burn mark and something locked into place behind my jaw. My voice came out quiet and sharp, a blade wrapped in cotton.

I overheard my in-laws talking about me in the next room. They said I was not good enough for their daughter. That I would never amount to much. I stood in the hallway with a glass of water in my hand, frozen. The glass was shaking. I set it down carefully, very carefully, because the alternative was throwing it at the wall.

The school called to say another kid had been bullying my son again. He came home with a torn jacket and would not look at me. I knelt down and saw the bruise on his arm. My vision went red at the edges. I stood up slowly. My whole body was coiled tight. I wanted to drive to that kid's house and put my fist through the door.

I filed the complaint six months ago and nothing happened. The company dumped chemicals into the creek behind our neighborhood and the response was a form email thanking me for my concern. I drove past the creek and the water was brown. Dead fish floated near the bank. I parked and got out and stood there trembling, my fists in my pockets, pressure building behind my eyes.

She said she would pick up the kids and she did not. They waited at school for an hour. When I got there, my son was sitting on the curb with his backpack, his chin on his knees. I loaded them into the car, drove home, and called her. She said she got busy. Got busy. I bit the inside of my cheek until I tasted copper.

I poured two years into the startup and my partner sold his shares without telling me. I found out from a lawyer's letter. He cashed out while I was still working sixteen-hour days. I read the letter three times. Each time the words hit harder. I threw my coffee mug at the wall and watched it shatter. The brown stain dripped down the white paint like a wound.

The driver ran the red light and nearly hit me in the crosswalk. I felt the wind off the bumper. He honked at me like I was the problem. I slammed both hands on the hood of the next car and stood there, chest heaving, adrenaline screaming through me. My whole body was shaking and I could not tell if it was from the near miss or the pure blinding fury.

I came home to find my roommate had given away my cat while I was traveling. She said the cat was too loud and she found a nice home. I stood in the doorway with my suitcase and the floor dropped out from under me. Then the heat hit. It rose from my stomach to my throat. I could not speak for a full minute. When I finally did, my voice did not sound like mine.

I spent the whole afternoon debugging code that someone else broke and did not mention. They pushed the change on Friday and left for vacation. I traced the bug through four files. Each one made my jaw tighten more. By the time I found the root cause, my neck was a knot of steel. I fixed it, wrote a commit message that was professionally worded but dripping with venom.

The airline canceled my flight and offered a voucher. Not a rebooking, a voucher. I had a meeting in six hours. I stood at the counter and watched the gate agent shrug. My voice stayed even but my knuckles were white on the counter edge. I asked to speak to a supervisor and was told none were available. I could feel my heartbeat in my clenched jaw.

My father made the same dismissive comment he always makes. I am forty years old and he still talks to me like I am twelve and stupid. I sat at Thanksgiving dinner with my fork in my hand, every muscle tense. My wife squeezed my knee under the table. I said nothing. I chewed my food mechanically. Inside, something old and hot was pressing against the walls of my chest.

I opened my locker and someone had gone through my things. My lunch was eaten, my water bottle was empty, and my jacket was crumpled in a ball at the bottom. I stood there staring at the mess. Heat crawled up the back of my neck and settled behind my eyes. I closed the locker door slowly, pressing it shut with one finger. The metal dented slightly under my grip.
